entries.intro: The merchant caravans that used to pass through {subject_focus_the} have stopped coming. Last three were supposed to arrive weeks ago.
details: We rely on those caravans for iron, cloth, and spices. Our own stores are running thin.
A rider came through saying the southern road is still open, but the merchants won't say why they've rerouted.
The last caravan leader who came through was nervous. Kept looking behind him and left before dawn.
reactions: We can manage without spices. Iron is another matter. The smith is rationing what he has.
If the caravans don't come back soon, we'll have to send our own people to the trading post. That's a five-day journey each way.

statCheck.pass: I spoke with a peddler who still travels the route. He said the merchants aren't afraid of bandits: they're afraid of something at the river crossing. He wouldn't elaborate, but he's been fording upstream instead.
statCheck.fail: Everyone has a different theory about the caravans and none of them have firsthand information. I have asked around plenty myself.

entries.intro: A new trader set up near {subject_focus_the} selling goods nobody can identify. Beautiful things, but nobody knows what they're made of or where they come from.
details: The fabrics shimmer like silk but they're tougher than leather. The dyes don't fade, even in rain.
He pays in standard coin but won't say where he sources his stock. Just smiles and says it's a trade secret.
His prices are suspiciously fair. Almost too good. The established merchants are losing customers fast.
reactions: I bought a scarf from him. It's the finest thing I've ever owned and it cost less than a meal. Something about that doesn't sit right.
The old merchants are furious. They're calling for the council to inspect his goods, but nobody can find anything actually wrong with them.

entries.intro: The grain prices near {subject_focus_the} have tripled in two weeks, and it's not because of a bad harvest. Someone is buying up the supply.
details: Three wagons of grain left the village heading north two days apart. Bought at market rate, sold at who knows what.
The buyer is a woman nobody recognizes. She pays in gold, not silver. Nobody around here uses gold for grain.
She's not hoarding it locally: the wagons don't come back. The grain is going somewhere else.
reactions: We'll have enough if we're careful, but the poorer families are already feeling the squeeze.
I don't mind someone buying grain. I mind someone draining our supply and vanishing with it. That feels like a setup.

statCheck.pass: I followed one of the wagons at a distance. It didn't go to any town on the north road. It turned off onto a track I didn't know existed, heading toward the mountains.
statCheck.fail: The north road is well-traveled. The wagon tracks disappear into the general traffic almost immediately.

entries.intro: The blacksmith near {subject_focus_the} received a shipment of ore she didn't order. Arrived on a cart with no driver, no invoice, no markings.
details: The ore is high quality: better than anything she normally gets from her suppliers. Practically smelts itself.
There's a symbol stamped on each ingot: a hammer inside a circle. She doesn't recognize the maker's mark.
She's used some of it already. Says the metal works differently: holds an edge longer, takes less heat to shape.
reactions: Free materials sound good until you wonder who wants a blacksmith in their debt.
She's half-grateful and half-suspicious. Smart woman. I'd feel the same way.

entries.intro: Trade at {subject_focus_the} has shifted to barter because nobody trusts the coin anymore. Three people found their silver pieces turning black overnight.
details: It's not tarnish: the metal itself is changing. The coins lose weight and become brittle.
Only coins from the last few months are affected. Older silver is fine.
The assayer tested a bad coin and said the silver content is less than half what it should be. Someone's been debasing the mint.
reactions: We've gone back to trading goods for goods. It works, but it's slow and it's hard to agree on fair value.
Whoever debased the coins made a lot of money in the short term. The rest of us pay for it now.

statCheck.pass: I compared the bad coins closely. They all have a tiny notch on the rim that the good coins don't: a filing mark. Someone is swapping real coins for counterfeits and marking which ones they've touched.
statCheck.fail: The bad coins look identical to the good ones except for the discoloration. Whatever the difference is, it is not visible to the eye.
